content.full_text:
Cálifornia
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 4,
1993
Thomas Biesek
South Pasadena Taxpayers Association
324 Hawthorne Street
South Pasadena,
CA
91030
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93—185
Dear Mr. Biesek:
You have requested advice on behalf of the South Pasadena
Taxpayers Association (herein referred to as
“SPTA”)
conàerning
the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (“Act”).
1
?
OUESTION
Will
SPTA incur campaign reporting requirements if it raises
money to pay for mailers opposing the development of a benefit
a.:,sessment district and a fire suppression district?
CONCLUS ION
Money raised or spent by SPTA to gather petition signatures
to protest the district assessments are not reportable until one
of the issues is placed on an election ballot.
FACTS
SPTA is an informal political watchdog group that monitors
1ccal municipal issues.
Currently, the organization is not
registered as a committee and does not expend or raise monies.
However,
SPTA has filed as a committee in prior years.
SPTA
wishes to start raising money for a mailer which would include a
“protest form” to defeat a proposed benefit assessment district.
If a certain percentage of property owners sign the protest
petitions, the district assessment tax would automatically be
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Codeof Regulations
Section 18000,
et q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box $07
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


A—93--185
Page
2
defeated.
According to our telephone conversation on May 17,
1993, if a certain percentage of property owners do not sign the
petitions, the tax becomes effective.
In either case, the issue
does not become a ballot measure before the voters.
In addition,
SPTA is opposed to the formation of a fire suppression district.
However,
under the relevant property owner protest rules if a
certain number of property owners .ign prntDst petitions, the City
Council must decide either to place the issae on an election
baflt or to terminate the tax propoa1.
Attached to your letter of April 28,
1993,
was a newsletter
that SPTA was considering mailing to taxpayers.
The newsletter
asks the public to contact their legislators and express support
for two legislative bills.
You stated in our telephone
conversation on May 17,
1993, that SPTA would not incur $5,000 in
expenditures for this publication or similar publications in a
calendar quarter.
ANALYSIS
The Act defines a measure as:
any constitutional amendment or other proposition which is
submitted to a popular vote atan election by action of a
legislative body,
or which is submitted or is intended to be
submitted to a popular vote at. an cJection by initiative,
referendum or recall procedure whether or not it qualifies
for the ballot.
Section 82043.
SPTA is planning to raise anc .pend money to defeat proposals
for a benefit assessment district
ind a fire suppression district
through the use of protest petitions.
Because the petition
processes do not qualify either o.. th issues for the ballot,
SPTA
has no campaign reporting obligations.
(In re Fontana
(1976)
2
FPPC Opinions
25 and Herzig Advice IeLter,
No. A—88-060.)
2
/
If the City Council places the tire suppression district
issue on an election ballot, all contributions received and
expenditures made for the purpose of influencing the voters for or
against the measure must be disclosed if
SPTA receives or expends
$1,000 or more in a calendar year.
(Sections 82015,
82025,
84200
and 84200.5.)
In addition, if
SPTA provides to any other
2/
Copies of Commission regulations and opinions are available
in many
law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials
and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission
at a cost of
10c per page.


A—93—185
Page
3
committee,
including a sponsored committee,
anything of value,
such as research, mailing lists,
or opinion poii results,
which
was obtained prior to the time that the City Council places the
issue on the ballot,
the fair market value of the items will be
deemed nonmonetary contributions to the committee.
(Section 82015
and Carmy Advice Letter,
No.
A—87—19l.)
An organization that makes payments of $5,000 or more in a
ca).€nar
quarter to influence state 1egiz1.tive action must file a
lhbying disclosure statement
(Form
6’15).
(Section 86115.)
A
payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging other
persons to contact legislative officials concerning pending
legislation is considered a “payment to influence” legislative
action.
(Section 82045(e).)
According to the facts,
SPTA has not
met the requisite $5,000 threshold for filing lobbying disclosure
statements.
Please do not hesitate to contact me at
(916)
322-5662 if you
have additional questions concerning this matter.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
Genal Couns1
by:
Lynda Ccssady
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Will
SPTA incur campaign reporting requirements if it raises
money to pay for mailers opposing the development of a benefit
a.:,sessment district and a fire suppression district?

CONCLUSION: Money raised or spent by SPTA to gather petition signatures
to protest the district assessments are not reportable until one
of the issues is placed on an election ballot.
